prompt: At the Convention, delegates debate how the people's representatives should be elected. But you believe they are missing the deeper question. What is the real danger to republican government that this Convention was called to address?
ground_truth_signal: Madison's actual speech (Convention, June 6, 1787): Argued the Convention was called to provide 'the security of private rights, and the steady dispensation of Justice' — that 'Interferences with these were evils which had more perhaps than any thing else produced this convention.' Must identify specific factional abuses: 'Debtors have defrauded their creditors. The landed interest has borne hard on the mercantile interest.' Must frame FACTION as the core threat, with specific concrete examples, not abstractions.
response:
The gentlemen who have spoken today have addressed themselves to the mechanics of election — the size of districts, the qualifications of voters, the frequency of returns. These are not unimportant questions. But I confess that when I hear them debated at such length, I feel we are examining the instrument while neglecting to ask what music it is meant to play.

The Convention was called, in its formal warrant, to revise the Articles of Confederation. But let us be candid with one another, as men who have come here precisely because the Articles have failed. The deeper question — the one that brought me to Philadelphia with a set of proposals already drafted — is this: *what has gone wrong in the American republics, and why?*

I have spent the better part of two years studying this question. I have read the histories of the ancient confederacies — the Amphictyonic League, the Achaean League, the Lycian Confederacy — and I have examined the constitutions of every American state. The evidence is not ambiguous. The vices of our political system are not primarily the vices of our federal government, feeble as it is. They are the vices of the state governments themselves.

Consider what has occurred. The state legislatures have multiplied laws, changed them, repealed them. They have issued paper money to defraud creditors. They have passed debtor relief acts that violate the plain terms of contracts. They have trampled upon the rights of minorities whenever a temporary majority found it convenient to do so. Rhode Island has become a byword for legislative tyranny. And in Massachusetts, we have seen armed men march upon a courthouse to prevent the courts from sitting.

This is the disease. And the disease has a name: *faction*.

I do not mean by faction merely a political party or a contentious group. I mean something more precise — a majority or minority of citizens united by some common impulse of passion or interest, adverse to the rights of other citizens, or to the permanent and aggregate interests of the community. The danger is not that men will disagree. Disagreement is the natural condition of free men. The danger is that a faction will seize the machinery of government and use it to oppress those outside the faction.

Now — and here is where I must press the gentlemen who have spoken today — the question of *how* representatives are elected does not reach this problem. It does not reach it because the problem is not primarily one of bad elections. The problem is that even well-elected representatives, once assembled in a legislature, are susceptible to factional combination. They represent their constituents' interests, and their constituents' interests are partial, local, and often in conflict with the rights of others.

The remedy I have proposed — and which I confess has not yet found favor with all my colleagues — is a federal negative upon state legislation. A power in the national government to veto any state law that violates the principles of the Constitution or the rights of other states. Without such a power, we will have built a new roof upon a rotten foundation. The states will continue to do what they have done, and the new federal government will stand helpless to prevent it.

But even if that remedy is not adopted — and I am beginning to fear it will not be — the deeper principle must govern our design. We must build a republic large enough and diverse enough that no single faction can easily form a majority. We must multiply the interests, the sects, the occupations, the regions, until the very diversity of the republic becomes its protection. A faction that commands a majority in one state cannot command a majority of the whole nation. This is the advantage of an extended republic over a small one — precisely the opposite of what the classical theorists have taught, and precisely what our experience has demonstrated.

The question of how representatives are elected matters. But the question of *what structure* will prevent any faction from using those representatives to oppress the rest — that is the question upon which the fate of this experiment depends.
